The development of mechanically tunable hydrogels that replicate the dynamic mechanoelastic properties of native extracellular matrices (ECMs) is essential for advancing 3D tissue engineering. DNA, with its precise, programmable architecture and exceptional control at the nanometre scale, offers a valuable platform for designing ECM-mimicking scaffolds. This study presents stiffness-tuneable DNA supramolecular hydrogels with different branching architectures for programming cellular and organellar states. Utilizing precise DNA motifs—including DX (Double Crossovers), PX (Paranemic Crossovers), and Tensegrity architectures—we engineer hydrogels with widely adjustable mechanical properties (50–185 kPa) without chemical additives or enzymatic crosslinking. These hydrogels exhibit excellent strain-bearing and load-bearing capacity, making them suitable for biomedical applications. Additionally, these DNA hydrogels influence cellular behaviour in retinal pigment epithelial (RPE1) cells by enhancing cellular adhesion, encouraging elongation (a 3–8-fold increase in area compared to control), and improving viability (dependent on concentration, 1–8-fold increase vs. control), while also maintaining organellar homeostasis, including mitochondrial fragmentation and ER stress reduction. This work presents a framework for automating the production of stiffness-tunable DNA hydrogel scaffolds, aligning with the mechanical needs of various cells and tissues, thereby advancing personalized, high-throughput tissue engineering platforms.

Hydrogels have already proven their indispensable role in tissue engineering by providing a three-dimensional, porous, and hydrated ECM-mimicking microenvironment for the comprehensive growth and development of cells and tissues1. Moreover, conventional hydrogels like collagen and alginate lack the mechanical tunability required to match the mechanoelastic needs of diverse human tissues (e.g., brain and adipose tissues: ~0.1–1 kPa; cartilage: ~10–25 kPa; skin: ~10–100 kPa; bone: >1 MPa)2,3. To modulate the mechanical stiffness of these conventional hydrogels, the addition/removal of polymers, linkers, bridging agents, or other materials is required. For example, in an attempt to create a void-forming hydrogel, Huebsch et al. proposed removing solid-phase porogens through hydrolysis after post-polymerization of the gel4. In this specific case, these solid-phase porogens were removed to modulate the mechanoelastic properties of the void-forming gels. Another example includes an attempt to fabricate superstrong, superstiff, and conductive alginate hydrogels by Ji et al. in 2022. They proposed to achieve this by densely connecting an interconnected network via an anisotropic densification of the pre-gel, followed by ionic crosslinking mediated by rehydration5. There are many other case scenarios where the mechanical or elastic properties of hydrogels have been tweaked, but at the cost of either the addition or removal of some macro- or micro-molecules. Molecules like DNA, protein, and carbohydrates can store information as well as to form diverse complex structures based on their specific shape, size, and information; however, amongst these, DNA offers a unique combination of ease of synthesis, high stability, and, most notably, precise structural programmability via base-pairing rules. These limitations often hinder the use of conventional hydrogels in regulating cell fates, influencing their adhesion, differentiation, and organellar functions in response to the mechanoelasticity of the scaffolds6,7.

All conventional polymeric hydrogels can regulate their mechanical stiffness through various modifications, including, but not limited to, increasing polymeric concentrations8, increasing molecular weights9, the addition of chemical/enzymatic crosslinkers10,11,12, or physical assistance by adding nanomaterials13. However, many of these techniques that are used to modify the mechanical stiffness of the hydrogel matrix also alter the physicochemical properties, accompanied by topological changes of the polymer in focus, which are extremely important for cellular functions14,15,16,17,18. These alterations include changes in matrix density, porosity, or reliance on amorphous synthetic materials, which can cause unavoidable cytotoxicity. These complications can be avoided by incorporating DNA polymers to form a Hydrogel network. The DNA strands are well-characterized to form predictable Watson-Crick-based Hydrogen Bonding with complementary nucleobases. The biological significance of DNA makes it very stable and inherently cytocompatible in nature compared to other polymers. Because of this reliable base-pairing, it is now possible to design a 3D structure from DNA19,20,21,22,23,24, as was first elaborated by Dr. Ned Seeman25,26,27. Following this development, many researchers followed suit and explored various capabilities of DNA nanostructures in various areas of science, particularly in biomedical applications28,29,30,31,32, neuroscience33,34, cellular programming27,35,36,37,38, therapeutics39,40, and tissue engineering41. Dan Luo et al. explored the potential of DNA to form extrinsically interlinked 3D scaffolds, forming DNA hydrogels.

DNA hydrogels offer unique advantages, both in form and function, by their inherent biocompatibility, biodegradability, along with biological functionality coupled with atomic-level precision through sequence design25,26,42,43,44,45,46,47. Recent advancements have demonstrated their efficiency and potential in providing ECM-mimicking environments for tissue engineering48,49,50,51,52,53. The fabricated hydrogel with ECM-mimicking properties should also provide ways for cells to modulate ECM regulation. A hydrogel system that does not degrade in response to the cellular needs also affects the cells during normal homeostasis and developmental phases18. For example, a study conducted by Zhou et al. found that the hydrogel stiffness alone may not be sufficient to drive the differentiation of NPCs. The interaction of the biomaterial with the cells drives differentiation, where the stiffness of the gel can be adjusted by the cells growing within it. Many research groups have demonstrated diverse ways of forming DNA-based hydrogels with mechanical stiffness in the range of 100 Pa–100 kPa52,54,55,56,57,58. The growing interest in DNA hydrogels stems from their inherent biological properties and ease of customization59,60,61,62,63,64, offering a platform for modulation of cellular responses in ways that conventional hydrogels cannot.

The architecture of DNA hydrogels is fundamentally determined by the interconnectivity of constituent DNA junctions, which regulate the branching architecture and stiffness of the hydrogels. At the molecular level, these junctions serve as a structural node that defines the network topology. The properties of hydrogel can be programmed by regulating these junctions. In our previous work, we tried to explore the role of soft DNA hydrogels made from branched simple DNA duplex junctions in programming the cellular behaviour of RPE1 cells, including enhanced cellular spreading, elongation, endocytic uptake, perturbation of ECM-regulating surface markers, mitochondrial fragmentation, and focal adhesion complex regulation52,53. These hydrogels exhibited a mechanical range of 0.1 Pa to 10 Pa, without the addition of crosslinkers or chemical modifications. Extending the scope of our previous work, reinforcing simple duplex junctions with mechanically robust and topologically stable junctions may serve as a key to unlock more features for DNA-based hydrogels in designing scaffolds for automated 3D bioprinting while systematically probing mechanobiology.

While prior studies have explored DNA hydrogels for drug delivery or biosensing, their potential as a mechanically tunable scaffold for tissue engineering applications remains underexplored. The integration of more complex DNA motif structures, such as i-motifs, G-quadruplexes, and DNA crossovers, into junctions may lead to diversification of geometry and mechanical properties. DNA motifs such as double-crossovers (DX) contain two crossover sites between helical domains, while a paranemic-crossover (PX) offers an even more complex design, featuring a four-stranded coaxial DNA complex with crossovers happening at every possible point65. These structures must be designed while keeping the topology of DNA strands in mind, where crossovers are separated by specific half-turns to avoid torsional stress. The increased structural rigidity of DX, and even so for PX, compared to simple duplexes, makes them valuable building blocks for constructing more stable DNA nanostructures and hydrogels65,66. Increasing the degree of sustained crossovers is one way to increase the rigidity of the hydrogels.

Another significant advancement is the development of hydrogels with tensegrity architectures inspired by the mechanochemical principles underlying cell-ECM interactions67,68,69. These motifs are made from ssDNA strands forming an isolated rigid compression unit, held in place by a continuous network of tensile elements, enabling tensile moduli up to 30 MPa69. These compression units act as a junction point, where, depending on the branching numbers, it is possible to vary the mechanical stiffness of the hydrogel network so formed. The incorporation of Tensegrity units into DNA hydrogel design represents a biomimetic approach to create a robust, adaptive platform for advanced mechanoregulated tissue engineering67,68,69.

We hypothesize that the degree of branching complexity in a DNA hydrogel network, via regulating the degree of crossovers and changing the number of branching arms (DX to PX to Tensegrity (trimers-hexamers)), directly governs hydrogel stiffness, which in turn can modulate cellular mechanotransduction pathways. To address the above hypothesis, we envisioned a set of DNA hydrogel networks, each varying in its precise junction architecture and branching arms. Herein, we demonstrate the design, synthesis, characterization, and applications of DNA hydrogels utilizing DX, PX, and tensegrity motifs (with 3-6 arms) in programming the morphology, viability, and organellar dynamics of RPE1 cells in a mechanotransduction-dependent manner (Fig. 1).

Representation of free and polymerized DNA supramolecular assemblies of Double Crossovers (DX), Paranemic Crossovers (PX), and Tensegrity Structures, enhancing cellular spreading, cell migrations, proliferations, mitochondrial and endoplasmic reticulum reprogramming.

Previously, we have reported how simple DNA duplexes could be modified to form soft DNA hydrogels for cell programming (Fig. 2.a). Moving forward from simple, multiarmed DNA complexes to higher-order crossover and tensegrity structures (Fig. 2.a), our goal is to generate a range of mechanically diverse DNA hydrogels that cater to the cellular mechanoelastic needs for optimal growth and development. In this study, we designed fourteen DNA motifs, including three structures of the DX type, three structures of the PX type, and two structures of the TT, TQ, TP, and TH types, respectively. Among these, each group has its own intrinsic control motif, demarcated as a truncated variant; hence, they do not form a DNA hydrogel network. These truncated motif structures are represented by the ‘0th' order, and the structures are DX0, PX0, TT0, TQ0, TP0, and TH0, respectively. The other DNA motifs have extended terminal ends, which facilitate network formation by sticking to their complementary sequence; hence, they are called sticky ends. These sticky ends act as a linker, bridging two similar DNA motifs (nodes) to form a network with each other. Depending on the sequence, these sticky ends can be of two types: palindromic and non-palindromic. The palindromic-type sequences are read the same from either direction, which enables the motifs to bind in any order, and represents a higher degree of freedom in terms of binding. The non-palindromic type of sticky ends can only bind in a sequence oriented in the 5' to 3' direction, giving a specific binding pattern.

a Illustrates various DNA topological motifs to form (i) Duplexes, (ii) Tensegrity structures; (iii) Doub2le crossovers, and (iv) Paranemic crossovers, respectively. b Shows schematics of the strategy implemented to generate sequences for the DNA motifs.

The Design process of these DNA hydrogels is exclusively based on the Watson-Crick base pairing rule. Here, we exploit the specific nature of nucleotide base-pairing to our advantage in designing structures with DX, PX, and tensegrity motifs. The sequence design pipeline follows several rules (Fig. 2.b): 1. Each crossover should be separated by integral half-turns of helical coil, to avoid torsional strain. 2. Incorporation of AT-rich sequences near the crossover sites and GC-rich sequences near the terminal ends ensures thermodynamically stable structures. 3. Sequences should be checked to avoid the potential for forming secondary structures or heterodimers. 4. Pair-wise alignment of ssDNA strands must be checked to calculate the minimum Gibbs Free energy (ΔG). 5. Visualization of assembled structures through scadNANO (2D assemblies) and oxDNA (3D assemblies) must be ensured (Supplementary Fig. 2). 6. Finalized sequences should be immunologically inert (checked via BLAST tools).

We designed fourteen unique DNA nanostructures (Supplementary Fig. 1), eight of which can form a network that forms a hydrogel in aqueous medium, while the remaining six serve as truncated controls (devoid of forming any networks). We also explored the nature of the sticky-arms in regulating the rigidity of the DNA hydrogels. As a result, we have three variants of DX: namely, DX0 (truncated motif), DX1 (DX motif with non-palindromic sticky end linkers), and DX2 (DX motif with palindromic linkers) (Supplementary Fig. 1.a). The presence/absence of palindromic linkers affects the scaffold stiffness by regulating the degree of freedom in which sticky-end-based joining is allowed. Palindromic linkers offer higher degrees of freedom compared to the sequence-confined non-palindromic linkage. Similar to DX, there are also three variants of PX, including PX0 (truncated control), PX1 (PX motif—non-palindromic linker type), and PX2 (PX motif—palindromic linker type) (Supplementary Fig. 1.b). However, for tensegrity structures, we decided to only incorporate non-palindromic linkers, thereby giving two variants of Tensegrity triangles (TT0, TT1) (Supplementary Fig. 1.c), Quadrilaterals (TQ0, TQ1) (Supplementary Fig. 1.d), Pentamers (TP0, TP1) (Supplementary Fig. 1.e), and Tensegrity Hexamers (TH0, TH1) (Supplementary Fig. 1.f).

We propose a mechanoelastic programmability within these DNA motifs through potential crossovers and tensegrity profiles, which in turn are directed by sequence-specificities. Theoretically, DX-type hydrogels would be softer than PX-type, as the latter contains multiple crossover sites, making them considerably stiffer than DX-type. We also expect that the tensegrity structures would show even greater stiffness values compared to PX-types, because they contain isolated compression units, with stringent tension between the assembled DNA strands. As we increase the number of branching arms in Tensegrity structures, we can derive stronger DNA scaffolds, resulting in hydrogels arranged in the order of decreasing stiffness from TH to TT.

The ssDNA strands were mixed in the desired stoichiometric ratio, as illustrated in the Supplementary Table 1, in the presence of 50 mM MgCl2 ions to form DNA hydrogels. The sequence-directed assembly of ssDNA strands to form the desired DNA hydrogels was driven by a thermal annealing process in a thermocycler/ PCR machine. The non-specific base-pairing of the strands is minimized at higher temperatures (~95 °C). Following this, the temperature is slowly reduced (−5 °C/ step) in a stepwise manner to guide the strands to anneal in a sequence-dependent manner. At the end of the reaction (~4 °C), the desired DNA strand binding state is achieved. However, we have observed that a prolonged overnight incubation increases the yield of the DNA hydrogels.

To test the formation of DNA hydrogels directed by specific linker sequences, we performed an electrophoretic shift assay (EMSA) on a 10% Native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) setup (Fig. 3; their raw files in Supplementary Fig. 10.a-m. The electrophoretic mobility shift assay is a gel-based electrophoresis method in which the sample is passed through a well-defined resolving gel system (e.g., polyacrylamide or agarose) under the influence of an external electric field, thereby providing a sieving effect on the sample. The DNA hydrogel samples were loaded in a systematic manner, with ssDNA loaded to the left of the DNA ladder lane and the subsequent assembly of these ssDNA strands shown in a cascading manner to the right. The higher the level of assembly, the bulkier the complex becomes, which causes it to take longer to pass through the matrix. However, the overall movement of DNA bands is governed by a net charge-to-mass ratio and the frictional coefficient of the complex against the porous gel. The DNA strands bound to their intended counterparts show sequential binding affinities, which further increase the mass of the complex. In specific cases, these complexes show co-migratory movements, indicating that the supramolecular 3D shape of the complex also plays an important role.

On the left side of the DNA ladder, color-coded ssDNA are loaded, which forms higher order complexes in increasing order of assemblies on the right indicated by A, B, C, D, and E. Lane A, B, C, D and E denotes assembly of 1st strand, 1st and 2nd strand, 1st, 2nd and 3rd strand, 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th strand and 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th and 5th strands, respectively in a sequential manner.

The truncated variants of the DNA hydrogels (DX0, PX0, TT0, TQ0, TP0, and TH0) show a minimal shift in their band patterns to the right side of the DNA ladders (Fig. 3.a, d, g, i, k, m), due to their strand-directed hybridization under the influence of thermal annealing effects. The slight shift observed in the lanes on the right is due to the formation of the DNA motif (truncated) in a sequential manner. The comigration bands show a trade-off between the complex shape vs mass. As a general rule, bulkier masses exhibit slower band mobility compared to smaller complexes. However, the compact shapes of complexes allow them to move smoothly and more quickly compared to non-compact, open-flanked complex structures. All network-type DNA structures exhibit significantly larger band retardations compared to their single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) counterparts, indicating the formation of higher-order DNA complexes that hinder their electrophoretic movement through the tight mesh of the acrylamide gel. Apart from the shape and the mass, the extent of band retardation is also dependent on the stiffness of the DNA motif and the degree of polymerization. As the degree of polymerization increases and the hydrogel becomes stiffer, the band pattern will be more significantly hindered under an external electric field. Figure 3 and Supplementary Fig. 10 demonstrate that the linker-type DNA motifs successfully form higher-order DNA supramolecular complexes, which show a strong band retardation pattern compared to truncated motifs.

We observed that the hydrogel samples comprising DX and PX types migrate further within the resolving gel, exhibiting a consistent band smear. This occurs due to the denser consistency of the hydrogels, which hinders their migration within the electrophoretic gel. We also observed that tensegrity structure-based hydrogels do not show these band smears. This phenomenon could be explained by the following points: 1. Most of these tensegrity-motif-based hydrogels are denser and more compact than the resolving medium (10% polyacrylamide matrix), making them poorly transmissible within the acrylamide gel. 2. Post-electrophoretic staining using EtBr, accompanied by gentle shaking/rocking, dislodges the DNA hydrogel samples from the mouth of the comb of these lanes. As a result, we don't observe a smear-like band pattern in the case of stiffer DNA tensegrity structures. Through the visual inspection of band patterns alone, it is sufficient to say that the DX and PX hydrogel of type 2 network form complex and very dense hydrogels even at a loading volume of 3 µL of 10 µM DNA Hydrogel concentrations.

The lack of prominent and crisp band patterns in the EMSA assay made it less reliable for assessing the supramolecular assembly of stiffer hydrogels. The lack of band smear is directly dependent upon the mechanical stiffness of the hydrogel. Therefore, highly porous acrylamide gels (>10%) or agarose gels (>2%) should be preferred for aptly resolving the systemic assembly of DNA supramolecules forming stiffer hydrogels.

Alternatively, the formation of nucleic acid-based DNA hydrogels can be assessed using fluorescent microscopes with DNA-binding fluorescent probes. In this work, the DNA supramolecules were labelled with GreenR, a DNA-intercalating fluorescent dye with excitation and emission spectra at 495 nm and 523 nm, respectively. The GreenR-bound DNA hydrogels can reveal the morphology of the DNA supramolecular hydrogels (Fig. 4). The truncated DNA variants exhibit no signs of hydrogel features, as they prominently lack the sticky arms/linkers required for this. However, we observed that the truncated DNA motifs tend to aggregate, forming µm-sized, uniformly distributed DNA condensates. The probable reasons for the formation of condensates may be due to charge interactions between Mg2+ ions, which could partially neutralize the charges of the nucleotides, thereby inducing liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS)70. The µm-sized DNA condensates in the case of truncated DNA motif samples can act as a local hotspot of scaffold-like physiological microenvironment, which can modulate cellular behaviour slightly in 2D in-vitro applications. In normal physiological conditions, these condensates are washed off into the plasma or can be easily diluted out by the interstitial fluids. It is visibly evident that the size and the intensity of the condensates are dependent on the number of arms of DNA tensegrity structures, indicating a significant involvement of branching architectures in determining the fates of DNA condensates71. The potential of these electrostatically stabilized DNA condensates could be explored for topically administered drug delivery applications. The increasing complexity and the number of branching arms in these truncated DNA motifs could be utilized as a dendritic core with branching arm modulation and precision over signal amplification71. Agarwal et al. 2024 have keenly demonstrated that with reduced network valency, the rate of dissolution of DNA condensates increases, which also supports our observations71,72. Also, the higher branched units are bigger in size; therefore, they can attract similar truncated units to condense following LLPS and grow larger in size70.

The truncated versions of DNA (DX0, PX0, TT0, TQ0, TP0, TH0) show no signs of branching morphology, indicative of hydrogel formations. The truncated DNA variants show µm scale aggregates-like features indicative of formation of DNA condensates. The non-zero variants of DNA show diverse network forming hydrogels. The concentration of the DNA hydrogels was 1 µM and stained with 0.01% GreenR dye. The scale bar is 60 µm.

The DX1 and PX1 motifs (Fig. 4) with specific sticky ends/linkers (non-palindromic type) tailored to provide a directional assembly of motifs, offer a spatial directionality, and improved stiffness over palindromic sticky ends-type DX2 and PX2 counterparts (Fig. 4). The nature of these sticky ends controls the behaviour and distribution of the hydrogel network at the nanoscale, thereby offering precise control over engineering hydrogels that cater to specific cell lines for advanced 3D tissue culture. The crossover-based hydrogels, such as DX1, DX2, PX1, and PX2, form a loosely defined, clumped DNA hydrogel network as observed through confocal microscopy. The sequence-defined linear nature of these supramolecules dictates the polymerization in linear fashion.

We believe that these ultra-long stretches of DNA motifs could collapse over onto themselves, forming a vaguely defined loose-network cluster (as could be seen in Fig. 4). The DNA hydrogels formed by double crossovers, DX1 and DX2, have fewer crossover sites compared to the paranemic crossover types, PX1 and PX2, which can be visually confirmed by a loose, undefined, compared to a rather smaller and spaced-out hydrogel network structure. Lesser number of crossover sites allows more flexibility to the terminal ends of the supramolecular motifs, which translates to a wider-network formed hydrogels. On the contrary multiple crossover sites in paranemic DNA motifs make them behave comparatively stiffer, restricting their spread of the polymerized network. The tensegrity structures, however, show a well-preserved specific scaffold geometry because of their precise strand geometry stabilized by tension. These hydrogels represent a network archetype which are well-spaced, planar, and spread out evenly, due to the influence of higher crossover sites and the tightly compacted state of the Tensegrity structures. Figure 4 illustrates liquid mode imaging of the greenR-tagged DNA hydrogels and truncated controls, where parameters like solvent concentrations73, temperature74,75, humidity, solute concentrations75,76, and LLPS77 can affect the geometry profile of DNA hydrogels.

An appropriate strategy to rule out the effects of these parameters is to study the polymerization behaviour in its dried state, and at constant temperature and salt concentrations. We can address this problem by slow drying steps, which preserve the nanometre-scale assemblies of the polymeric hydrogel systems by eliminating the unpredictable interactions with water. We performed BioAFM-based morphological analysis of these DNA supramolecular assemblies and observed that the truncated controls exhibit similar condensate-like morphologies, consistent with our previous findings via confocal microscopy of greenR-tagged DNA (Fig. 5). As expected, and verified, the truncated DNA motifs do not exhibit any signs of assembly forming interconnected chains. But the incorporation of Sticky ends enables them to instantly form hydrogels. The DX1 and PX1 variants, which exhibit directional specificity due to their non-palindromic linker type, display a mix of condensed granular and fibre-like morphologies (Fig. 5.c, f). The 5' to 3' polarity of these sticky ends provides a spatial constraint over the association of vacant DNA motifs, offering controlled assembly of DNA strands. Random orientation of motifs, directed by the palindromic linkers or the lengthy core of the DNA motifs, gives rise to fibrous hydrogel features, where each fibre-like feature is composed of parallel stacking of multiple strands of the DNA network. The incorporation of tensile forces through the introduction of tensegrity structures enables the enhancement of mechanical properties, as reflected by the tight packing of DNA fibres into thicker, longer, and well-defined bundles of the DNA network (TT1, Fig. 5.i). However, this tight packing is only observed at the smaller tensile cores of the DNA motif. Therefore, such features are strictly limited to TT1-type hydrogels. Increasing the DNA motif core size by increasing the number of strands destroys the compact and straight fibrous assembly by inducing flexibility to the fibres, assuming an organic polymer mimicking morphology. The flexibility of the tightly packed DNA fibres is evident as the number of arms increases from three in TT1 to six in TH1. Interestingly, the TP1 and TH1 hydrogel network closely resembles the morphology of collagen-based hydrogels78. The morphological profiles of these mechano-customizable DNA hydrogels show morphological network dependencies on sequences, motifs, and network valencies, indicating a highly programmable scaffolding system for delivering bespoke mechanical cues for specific cell types.

Control consists of 50 mM of MgCl2 dissolved in NFW and drop-cast over a freshly cleaved mica sheet. The scale bars for truncated controls and non-truncated network-type hydrogels are 1 µm and 2 µm, respectively.

AFM-based force spectroscopic analysis of DNA hydrogels in liquid mode revealed the mechanical stiffness range of these DNA hydrogels to be in the range of 52 kPa to 185 kPa (Fig. 6). AFM-based Force spectroscopy is a dynamic technique that applies a small oscillatory indent on top of the standard force-distance curve, allowing us to measure the complex indentation modulus (E'). Based on these interactions, the amplitude and phase of the oscillating cantilever change in accordance with the adhesion forces exerted by the samples. These dynamic parameters, including the force-distance curves plotted by the ascent and descent of the cantilever, are used to calculate the viscoelastic response at the drive79,80. We used a Hertzian model for a spherical indenter to calculate the apparent Young's modulus or Elastic storage modulus(E') of the DNA hydrogels in their native hydrated state.

Each hydrogel sample was nanoindented in an oscillating AC mode, with scanning performed in a 32 × 32 grid spanning an area of 100 × 100 µm², resulting in 1024 points of nanoindentation per scan area. An average of five areas was quantified to yield the storage elastic modulus (E') of these hydrogel samples.

In this study, our primary focus is on understanding the motif-dependent, mechano-customizable potential of DNA-supramolecular hydrogels. In network-forming DNA motifs, the double crossover structures like DX1 (E' ~ 185.336 kPa) and DX2 (E' = 181.994 kPa) showed the highest stiffness compared to other groups. Owing to multiple crossover sites, PX-type hydrogels showed E' values in the range of 91 kPa-165 kPa. The trend of rigidity showed a decrease in hydrogel stiffness following the order of DX > PX> Tensegrity structures. The stiffness, however, showed a decline in triangular and quadrilateral tensegrity structures (TT1 and TQ1, respectively). The significant increase in the stiffness of tensegrity structures, from TT1 to TH1, can be attributed to the increased valency of polymeric arms, which enhances the degree of polymerization and consequently affects mechanical stiffness. To accurately probe the relationship between core supramolecular valency and the stiffness of its higher-order structures requires further exploration with higher-order tensegrity branching motifs that have valencies greater than 6. At lower odd valency, n = 3, that is, TT-type DNA hydrogels, show reduced stiffness due to the smaller compression units and reduced steric hindrances. The higher the valency and the greater the odd number of arms present in a structure, the greater the steric hindrance will be, which can further enhance the mechanical stiffness of the assembled network. This can explain the stiffness profiles in TT1, followed by TQ1 and TH1, with G' values of 60.59 kPa, 52.93 kPa, 93.68 kPa, and 113.64 kPa, respectively.

The measurements carried out by AFM-based force spectroscopy are direct and precisely measure the stiffness of the polymers at the nanometre scale. However, the cantilever fails to precisely apply any shearing deformation to the material. The most common interaction is through indented force curves, which follow Newtonian mechanics and hence only simplified Hertz-Sneddon models, which assume the material to be perfectly elastic, isotropic, and homogeneous.

The hydrogels, however, do not fit this description. As a result, bulk behaviour estimation is required, which can be aptly achieved through rheological studies. AFM-based force spectroscopy can provide an accurate estimation of the surface stiffness of polymeric hydrogels at the nanometre scale, due to the scale of the cantilevers used. The macroscopic mechano-elastic characterization can be achieved by rheometers. These rheological tests enable us to understand the holistic behaviour of DNA hydrogels under strain deformations and decouple their linear viscoelastic region and mechanical stiffnesses.

To probe the bulk mechano-elastic properties of DNA hydrogels, oscillatory rheology is preferred, which provides key insights into the material characteristics of the hydrogel at various amplitudes, frequencies, and shear strains. To assess the structural integrity and deformation limits of the DNA hydrogels, amplitude sweep tests were performed at a constant frequency of 6.28 rad/s or 1 Hz. As shown in Supplementary Fig. 3.a, the storage modulus (G') and the loss modulus (G”) of the hydrogel samples remained consistent with the increase in deforming shear strain below 1%, defining the linear viscoelastic region (LVR). This LVR plateau indicates a stable, solid-like network where the microstructure recovers elastically from the small deformations. Below 1% shear strain, the storage modulus of hydrogels remained significantly higher than the loss modulus, suggesting that at no or lower shearing strain levels, the materials represented a solid-network forming hydrogel response. Beyond the 1% strain levels, the controls, such as PLL, Collagen-I, and Geltrex, exhibit a sudden network breakdown, as indicated by the crossover of their G' and G” values. This point represents the critical strain or yield strain of the control samples, marking the transition from an elastic to a viscous domain-flow state. Interestingly, the critical strain point for the DNA hydrogel samples occurs at a much higher strain level (~30%). These DNA hydrogels show admirable persistence against the shearing deformation forces of the levels that our cells can tolerate within the human body. According to the literature, the minimal shear strain experienced by the cells of diverse tissue systems, like articulative cartilages (1–10%81, blood vessels (0.2–2%82,83), Bone (0.03–0.2%84,85), Lung parenchyma (2–12%86;), skeletal muscles (<1%87), and brain tissues (0.1–1%88) lies ~10% or lower strain levels. Clearly, the DNA hydrogels demonstrate superior retention of their mechanical stiffness, enabling them to withstand extreme strain conditions within the human body. These mechanical properties of the DNA hydrogels explored here make them highly appealing for biomedical and clinical applications.

At a 1% shear strain, the storage modulus values of all the hydrogels, along with the controls, are shown in Fig. 7, where all the substrate groups are compared with the PLL group, indicating significant mechanical stiffness at 1% strain levels. The observations support the robustness of DNA hydrogels against small deformations. Its yield point is governed by the Watson-Crick base pairing that stabilizes the hydrogel. The observed breakdown beyond 30% strain levels is most likely due to forced dissociation of hydrogen bonds rather than the rupture of covalent backbones89,90. This information could be leveraged to further strengthen DNA supramolecular assemblies through UV-based intra-strand crosslinking reactions, which convert most of the base-pairing H-bonds to covalent bonds.

The storage modulus values of the DNA hydrogel sample at a fixed shear strain of 1% studied through Amplitude Sweep at a constant frequency of 6.28 rad/s. At a 1% shear strain, the DNA hydrogels exhibit storage modulus values ranging from 100 to 200 pascals. A Simple One-way ANOVA was used to compare the results against the collagen-I-coated substrate (n = 3). **** signifies p < 0.0001.

The weak frequency dependence of Storage and loss modulus values of DNA hydrogels observed in Frequency sweeps is a hallmark of a physical hydrogel (Supplementary Fig. 3.b). The persistence of G' > G” across all measured frequencies indicates the capability of the hydrogel to maintain its structural integrity over the course of varied timescales relevant to biological processes spanning seconds to h. This quality is a prerequisite for drug delivery applications and as a 3D cell culture scaffolding material. The slight increase in G” within the 1 Hz and 10 Hz frequency range could be attributed to the viscous dissipation associated with the reconfiguration of DNA strands due to solvent friction within the network (due to increased hydrodynamic interactions as established by the Zimm model of unentangled polymer dynamics)91,92,93.

Compared to the common ECM mimics like Matrigel or Geltrex, our DNA hydrogels show a stiffness (G' ~ 117–180 Pa) that is significantly stiffer than the basement membrane, like Geltrex (Fig. 7). The programmable nature of the DNA supramolecular hydrogels enables mechano-elastic tunability independent of concentration, offering a distinct advantage over conventional hydrogels. The rheological profile, characterized by a solid-like response, a tunable yield strain, and long relaxation times, unlocks the potential use of these DNA-based supramolecular hydrogels for biomedical applications, drug delivery, and 3D tissue culture domains.

The morphology and rigidity of the hydrogel can be precisely programmed by adjusting the sequence context of the input strands. Understanding the specific stiffness profile of these motifs enables us to design a hydrogel network with a specific stiffness by combining diverse motifs to achieve synergistic mechanical properties. Due to the high precision of DNA as a biopolymer, it could be very quickly scaled up to form mechano-customizable hydrogels in bulk.

AFM probes a tiny volume compared to the bulk estimation of hydrogels by rheometers. The values of E' through AFM are often higher than those of G' (from bulk rheology) because the AFM probes are tiny and can accurately tap onto the polymer at the nanometre scale, providing higher sensitivity in estimating the stiffness profiles of local microstructures. The values of E' obtained through AFM are often higher than those of G' (from bulk rheology)93,94 because the AFM probes can accurately interact with discrete polymer chains, dense fibrillar bundles, or cross-linking nodes, providing a high-resolution map of microstructural heterogeneity and thus have a higher sensitivity in estimating the stiffness profiles of local microstructures. Also, the tiny AFM probes experience water drag during faster indentation studies. This poroelastic/hydraulic resistance adds as an extra time-dependent stiffening component to the AFM measurements. On the other hand, rheology averages the response of billions of these fibrillar networks and pores simultaneously, allowing it to ensemble average microscopic properties, which are dominated by the weakest links and the water-filled networks. The water-filled, porous structures of hydrogels allow incompressible water to flow within the networks during rheological studies, which contributes to lower elastic shear resistance and, hence, lower mechano-elastic strengths compared to AFM-based force spectroscopy.

In our opinion, both techniques are crucial to probe the intricacies of the mechanical and elastic properties of hydrogels. Corroborating the findings from these two diverse techniques enables us to gain a deeper understanding of material and tissue interactions, both at the macro- and nanoscales.

The DNA-based hydrogels are exceptionally biocompatible and bio-inert, making them the perfect biopolymeric material for cellular programming. Although the DNA strands do not exhibit cytotoxicity, it becomes crucial to understand the dynamic interplay between the stiffness-programmable DNA hydrogels and RPE1 cells. The cytotoxicity of the synthesized DNA hydrogels was thoroughly tested at three different concentrations: 25 µM (Supplementary Fig. 4.a), 50 µM (Fig. 8), and 100 µM (Supplementary Fig. 4.b). At lower concentrations of 25 µM DNA Hydrogels, the RPE1 cells showed an increase in the cell populations, as indicated by an increase in the absorbance of the formazan crystals formed during the MTT assay, compared to our control, i.e., poly-L-lysine-treated (0.01%) RPE1 cells. We have also cross-compared the effects of Collagen-I (1.5 mg/mL) and Geltrex (0.15 mg/mL)-based substrate coating to compare the efficacies of these DNA hydrogels. Collagen-I is one of the important components of the ECM, while Geltrex is the ECM substrate from the basement membrane and contains most of the biopolymers of the ECM. It would be interesting to compare the cell viability of RPE1 cells grown over DNA hydrogels with that of cells grown over Collagen-I and Geltrex, providing a good range of mechanical stiffness required by the cells. The reported Young's moduli of Geltrex and Collagen-I are 892 Pa95 and range from 0.5 to 12 kPa96, respectively.

A Simple One-way ANOVA was used to compare the results. **** signifies p < 0.0001.

At higher concentrations of 50 µM (Fig. 8), the cellular viability of RPE1 cells with respect to the treatment of the rigid DNA hydrogels was more pronounced, compared to the PLL-treated group, Col-I, and Geltrex. Interestingly, in the double-crossover hydrogel group, the DX0 group, which does not form a hydrogel network, exhibits comparable cell growth to that observed in the group with the highest growth rate, DX1. The exact reason why DX0 and PX0 condensates enhance cell viability is not fully understood. We propose that these truncated DNA controls provide miniaturized hotspots of soft-cushioning of a scaffold-like environment, thereby maximizing the cellular viability of RPE1 cells. We believe that this soft cushioning provides localized availability of media and growth factors sequestered from the surrounding. Comparing all the candidates of the DX type, the change in cell viability of RPE cells is non-significant. Nonetheless, these DX motifs exhibit threefold cellular viability compared to PLL, 1.5 times that of Collagen-I, and no significant difference between Geltrex substrate coatings.

At the highest concentration of 100 µM (Supplementary Fig. 4.b), DNA hydrogels show a phenomenally high cellular viability response compared to lower DNA HG concentration levels. On average, all DNA hydrogels, whether truncated or network-type, show a four-fold increase in cellular viability compared to PLL alone. The double-crossover type DNA systems provide the maximum cellular viability response. From the aspects of complexity and crossovers, it is expected that tensegrity structures have higher mechanical stiffness compared to DX and PX type hydrogels. And from the behaviour of RPE 1 cells, it is certain that RPE 1 cells appreciate hydrogels of a comparatively softer stiffness profile. This study sheds light on the dynamic interplay between DNA hydrogels and RPE1 cells, revealing a concentration-dependent response in cell viability.

Apart from the exceptionally high cellular growth, the mechano-customizable DNA hydrogels also modulate the morphology of the RPE1 cells in a stiffness-regulated manner. The RPE1 cells are epithelial cells with a generally spindle-shaped morphology on flat substrates, such as glass or plastic culture dishes. Stiffer substrates promote cytoskeletal organization and distribution, which apply traction forces to the cellular focal adhesion sites and thereby trigger the cells to contract. This process of stiffness-induced contraction allows the cells to become elongated and spindle-like (Supplementary Fig. 6). The stiffness of ECM also enhances the cellular area to become enlarged, compared to constricted growth over flat or soft substrates.

Among the controls used in this study, PLL consists of a layered aggregation of poly-L-lysine, which coats the substrate with a soft and fibrous layer. Collagen I is a highly purified component of the ECM and defines the stiffness of the tissues, including bones and cartilage. Collagen I cannot entirely represent the ECM alone. Geltrex is a commercially available cocktail of ECM components, including laminin, Collagen IV, entactin, and heparin sulfate proteoglycans, among others. The Geltrex can therefore supplement the RPE1 cells with overall growth conditions, as can be expected in an in-vivo site. The last control is a glass coverslip, which provides a micron-level surface for cells to anchor and grow in the presence of cell-culture grade media.

Figure 9 shows single-cell images, and Supplementary Fig. 7 shows wide-angle images of RPE 1 cells cultured over various substrates of varied stiffness at 50 µM concentrations. Upon visual inspection, it is evident that actin organization and assembly are slightly reduced in softer substrates, such as PLL, collagen-I, and Geltrex, compared to DNA hydrogel samples.

a Single-cell images of actin, nucleus, and mitochondrial expression and distribution. b, c Quantified and analyzed fluorescence data of actin and nucleus show stiffness-dependent cell and nucleus area enlargement in RPE1 cells. The scale bar is 20 µm. A simple one-way ANOVA was performed to compare the graphs. **** indicates p-value < 0.0001

Actin staining via phalloidin-tagged Alexa Fluor 488 dyes gives a rough estimation of cell shape, size, and area. Quantification of these samples reveals that these DNA hydrogels enhance the cells' spreading potential by modulating actin reorganization. We observed an increase in cell area for the cells grown over truncated control groups compared to the intact hydrogel network groups at lower mechanical stiffness levels. This phenomenon is more pronounced in stiffer hydrogels, such as TT1, TQ1, TP1, and TH1. We also observed that the change in cellular area for truncated and non-truncated DNA motifs is not significant. This observation could be explained by the specific mechanoelastic preference of RPE 1 cells and the local resource availability facilitation by the DNA condensates to the RPE-1 cells. Nanoindentation studies by Amela et al., for measuring the stiffness of cortical and nuclear cells of the eyes, reveal that the stiffness of these cells was within the range of 0.1 kPa to ~20 kPa. Another study highlighted the stiffness of corneal cells to be in the range of 7.5 kPa to 109.8 kPa97. This evidence has a direct correlation with the specific stiffness preference of the DNA hydrogels by the RPE1 cells. The increase in cellular area continues to rise along with an increase in the stiffness of DNA hydrogels, reaching the Young's modulus values of PX2 hydrogels, which are close to the innate stiffness of RPE1 cells. Increasing the scaffold stiffness resulted in a decreasing trend in cellular area. Furthermore, the stiffer hydrogels, such as TT1, TQ1, TP1, and TH1, offer an unfavourable microenvironment to the RPE1 cells, as indicated by a decline in cell area. However, the condensates of stiffer DNA motifs (Fig. 7) provide the necessary mechanical forces and resource localization required by RPE1 for optimal growth and development, which is reflected in an enhanced cellular area (Fig. 9.b) and Supplementary Fig. 5.a) compared to hydrogels of DX and PX junctions.

The nucleus of RPE 1 also shows a hydrogel stiffness-dependent mechanomodulatory response. RPE1 cells grown over hydrogels show significantly larger nuclei (~2–3x vs. PLL) (Fig. 9.c) and Supplementary Fig. 5.b). A normalized ratio of nucleus to cytoplasmic area reveals that the net increase in cell area during culture over DNA-hydrogel-coated substrates does not correspond to an equal increase in nuclear size (Supplementary Fig. 5.c). The abnormally high ratio of nuclei to cell area in the case of RPE1 cells grown over PX2 seems to be an anomaly and could occur due to sampling errors. However, Supplementary Fig. 5.d) provides a side-by-side comparison of cell area and nucleus area estimations of RPE1 grown over 50 µM of DNA hydrogels.

The regulation of ECM stiffness by programming various DNA hydrogel motifs can, in turn, regulate cytoskeletal organization, which subsequently affects cell morphology and organelle function. Increased cellular area and proliferation upon growth on DNA hydrogels indicate a metabolically active state of the cell, which creates pressure on normal homeostatic states of the cellular organelles. A faster rate of replication, coupled with an enlarged cellular morphology, is often associated with the rapid regulation of the cell's organellar distribution and organization. The endoplasmic reticulum and Mitochondria are two of the most widely studied cellular organelles, whose distribution and regulation have a strong correlation with ECM stiffness.

Activation of mechanotransducers, such as Piezo1, triggers Ca2+ influx via activation of ERK signalling pathways98,99. This led to mitochondrial fission, characterized by a reduction in tubular networks and a decrease in the fluorescence signal of the MitoTracker DeepRed dye. As a result, we observe a decrease in fluorescence signal corresponding to an increase in scaffold stiffness levels. Increased cellular proliferation diverts the cells' attention from oxidative phosphorylation to glycolysis, providing an instantaneous energy supply to meet the needs of actively dividing cells52,100. Fragmented mitochondria are more easily accessible for faster ATP turnover during cellular division, which supports our findings. All the DNA motifs show enhanced mitochondrial fragmentation (Fig. 10.b) in lieu of faster cellular division (Fig. 8). Comparing the cytocompatibility profile of DNA hydrogels and Mitochondrial staining, the mechanically customizable DNA hydrogels program the cells to divide faster, increasing metabolic activity, which in turn fragments the mitochondria to meet the cellular energetic demand. To reduce the influence of non-uniform Mitotracker deep red fluorescence levels, we have also analyzed the fluorescence flux of it. The flux is defined as the fluorescence per unit area of the cells and is a useful method for normalizing the fluorescence levels of any cell target to its average area in each experimental condition. To investigate the effects of mechano-customizable DNA hydrogels on RPE 1 cells, we calculated the fluorescence flux of the Mitotracker Deep Red dyes, averaged over the cell area. Supplementary Fig. 7.a shows the highest flux in the case of DX2, followed by Geltrex and the Tensegrity structures. The higher flux of MitoTracker dye in Geltrex-treated cells is due to the smaller cell area, which contains a ten-fold expression of MitoTracker dye compared to PLL. In general, the softer a matrix is, the faster the RPE 1 cells will grow, with an inversely downregulated MitoTracker flux level indicating an increase in mitochondrial network fragmentations.

The changes in ER expression levels also seem to be regulated by a mechanotransduction-driven ER structural remodelling and stress response attenuation101,102. Stiffer hydrogels produce cellular tractions through actomyosin contractions, resulting in the elongation of the cell, which would otherwise remain circular (Supplementary Fig. 6). These tensions are transmitted to the ER networks through mechanotransducers, such as Nesprins causing the stretching and expansion of ER tubules. This change is perceived as an increment in ER tracking fluorophores' signals.

We observed a positive correlation between increased MitoTracker expression patterns and hydrogel stiffness values in the range of 100 kPa. Once the scaffolds cross this 100 kPa threshold, the MitoTracker expression pattern decreases. Closer inspection of the single-cell images reveals that the fragmentation profile of mitochondria increases with an increase in stiffness of DNA hydrogels (Fig. 10.b). This could explain the increased fluorescence of fragmented mitochondria distributed throughout the cells. At higher stiffness values (>100 kPa), the RPE 1 cells exhibit a decrease in cellular area and proliferation, which correlates positively with a decrease in mitochondrial absorbance and an increase in filamentation of the mitochondrial networks (Supplementary Fig. 9.a).

Inversely, the ER tubules become elongated with increased cellular traction due to mechanotransductionary regulation from DNA hydrogels. Below the threshold of 100 kPa Young's moduli, the elongation in ER directly follows the trends of increased mechanoelasticity of the substrate; however, after crossing the threshold, the expression of ER tracker drops significantly (Fig. 10.c). The flux of the ER tracker reveals a stiffness-induced increase in flux; however, the Flux of the PLL conditions is significantly higher than that of any other group (Supplementary Fig. 9.b). The pattern of flux perturbations inversely follows the stiffness profiles of the DNA hydrogels. At higher stiffness levels, the ER tubules elongate and fuse with each other in the RPE 1 cells, whose area increases significantly in higher stiffness hydrogel conditions. As a result of this, the per-unit fluorescence of ER tubules decreases as the scaffold stiffness increases. This phenomenon is biologically relevant as increased cell area demands increased cytoplasmic resources, including proteins. The ER tubules fuse and enlarge to provide for the increased demand for cytosolic proteins and other packaged cargoes.

DNA supramolecular hydrogels offer unprecedented control over their integrated mechanostructural environment, in which higher branching complexity simultaneously alters both the nanoscale network architectures (pore size, network geometry, fibre length, thickness, etc.) and the resulting mechanical stiffness. The incorporation of DX, PX, and tensegrity structures, along with specific linker types, allowed us to create a range of diverse palette of DNA hydrogels with distinct network morphologies and tailored mechanical dynamics. We demonstrated a substantial increase in polymeric stiffness by incorporating tensegrity structures, where the mechanical stiffness reached a maximum of E' = 185.34 kPa of polymeric stiffness. Through this work, we demonstrated a viable framework for designing and synthesizing customizable DNA hydrogels with a wide range of stiffnesses from ~52 kPa to ~185 kPa (determined via force spectroscopy in liquid mode).

The rheological characterization of DNA hydrogels reveals a persisting LVR region, indicating a solid-like, stiffer hydrogel network with G' > G”. These hydrogels also exhibit shear-thinning behaviour, with stiffness decreasing with increasing shear strain, making them an ideal choice for intravenous/intradermal drug delivery applications. These hydrogels can withstand shearing stresses significantly higher than the maximum shearing forces that our body can tolerate, thereby highlighting their promising potential for biomedical and clinical applications. At higher-frequency sweeps, these DNA hydrogels also demonstrated the robustness of their polymeric networks, which could withstand a range of timescales relevant to biological processes while maintaining their structural integrity. These observations provide insight into programming DNA hydrogels with precise morphology and rigidity by adjusting the sequence context of the input strands. Understanding the specific mechanostructural profile of these motifs enables us to design a hydrogel network with synergistic mechanical properties, with a targeted preference for cell culture, drug delivery, personalized gene therapy, clinical, and biomedical applications. For more realistic organoids or 3D tissue cultures, a combination of two or more specific DNA motif types could be explored, with each motif supporting and nurturing cells that require varied ECM properties. Due to the high precision of DNA biopolymers, they can be quickly scaled up to form high-throughput mechano-customizable hydrogel systems for advanced tissue or organ printing applications in the near future.

These hydrogels have been shown to regulate cellular proliferation and behaviour, including adhesion, spreading, proliferation, shape, and organellar dynamics such as mitochondrial fragmentation and ER stress attenuation- driven by their unique integrated mechanostructural environments. A fundamental limitation in evaluating distinct structural motifs (DX, PX, Tensegrity) is that altering the DNA motif simultaneously alters the network architecture and bulk stiffness. Because overall morphology and pore size can independently regulate cellular spreading, it is challenging to attribute cellular responses to rigidity alone when comparing across different motifs. However, in our previous work, we have partially decoupled these variables by evaluating the cellular responses in a concentration-dependent manner103. By varying the concentration of a single motif type, we effectively modulated the bulk hydrogel rigidity and network density while keeping the fundamental junction architecture and binding geometry constant. As demonstrated, increasing the concentration significantly enhanced cellular area and actin polymerization, driving dose-dependent cell spreading and programmed reorganization of actin cytoskeleton fibres. This strongly indicates that, while motif-dependent network architecture plays an undeniable role, the matrix's bulk mechanical compliance is a dominant, causal driver of the observed cellular mechanotransduction. While our findings highlight hydrogel rigidity as a major driver of cellular behaviour, the concurrent influence of nanoscale network properties cannot be overlooked. Fully decoupling the distinct roles of bulk stiffness and matrix architecture presents a complex challenge. Therefore, systematically isolating these variables to map their individual effects on cell growth will form a central focus of our future investigations.

The RPE1 cells show an increment in cellular area when cultured over hydrogels with <100 Pa of Young's modulus stiffness. This observation is coordinated with an active remodelling of the ER reticulum, and Mitochondrial states in a stiffness-dependent manner. RPE1 cells start showing signs of reduced cell area coupled with mitochondrial filamentation and reduced ER signals upon exposure to hydrogels with E' > 100 kPa. With moderately stiff, well-spaced networks, mitochondrial fragmentation is accelerated, while ER tubules elongate, resulting in an overall increase in cell area. The underlying complex mechanosensory signalling needs to be thoroughly explored to shed light on the key regulatory proteins that orchestrate the cell-ECM interactions.

By systematically investigating the relationship between DNA structural complexity and derived mechanical properties, we have established a framework for the rational design of DNA hydrogels with precisely tuned stiffness and geometric features. The ability to program both the structure and function of these DNA hydrogels through sequence design paves the way for significant advancements over conventional hydrogel systems, offering new possibilities for creating instructive cellular microenvironments for precise modulation of cell behaviour and tissue development.

The ability to control both the mechanical properties and cellular responses through DNA hydrogel design opens numerous avenues for applications in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine. By matching the desired mechanical properties and native ECM architectures of the tissue through sequence optimizations, these hydrogels could be scaled up as an instructive scaffold system that not only supports cellular growth but also actively guides tissue development and homeostasis. The ease of customizability and functionalization of DNA scaffolds offers a lucrative opportunity to further modulate specific cellular responses.

The addition of stimulus-responsive motifs, such as G-quadruplexes, i-motifs, CpG regions, hairpin loops, and aptamers, can further extend the scope of this study. The incorporation of these genomically conserved unique motifs can be used to make DNA hydrogels dynamically responsive to real-time changes in temperature, pH, solute/solvent concentrations, and other environmental factors. This approach would more closely mimic the dynamic nature of the natural ECM, which undergoes continuous remodelling during tissue development and regeneration. Furthermore, using the cross-linking strategy, such as UV-based photopolymerization of intra-strand bases, makes the DNA hydrogels stiffer by replacing H-bonds with covalent bonds. The mechanical stiffness of these polymers can also be enhanced by crystallization using cationic salt complexes, such as Calcium and phosphate depositions over the negatively charged DNA backbone. The aggregation of these charged cations induces backbone-centric crystallization events, making the DNA hydrogels more rigid. The DNA strands comprising the hydrogels are randomly oriented in space, resulting in a local force-unloading event upon stress loading. The mechanical stiffness of such systems can be significantly enhanced by aligning the polymers in a desired direction, i.e., by arranging the polymeric strands of DNA in an anisotropic manner. The work carried out by Mu et al. exactly elaborated this scheme. They have developed anisotropic DNA hydrogels with precisely unidirectional void pores104. Their research demonstrated that unidirectional channels in DNA hydrogels significantly enhance the responsiveness of the gel to biomacromolecular materials, while also imparting anisotropic qualities, including anisotropic mechanical properties, anisotropic swelling/shrinking behaviours, and facilitating anisotropic cell migration.

Furthermore, the DNA strands can be chemically modified to express ligands with nanometre-level spatial arrangement, which can drive cellular differentiation at desired time points upon cellular interactions with multiple ligands distributed specifically across the hydrogel scaffold. This strategy could be employed to develop a robust platform for studying head-tail polarity or more complex, holistic tissue development in developmental biology. These hydrogels, owing to their precision and programmability, are most suitable for personalized medicine in the future, where the mechanical properties of these hydrogels can be tailored to meet the specific needs of individual patients. By integrating patient-derived cells with custom-designed DNA hydrogels, it would become possible to create more accurate and efficient disease models for testing drugs and developing personalized therapeutic regimens.

We have demonstrated pure DNA-junction-based hydrogels with customizable mechanoelastic properties. In the future, we aim to explore the photodynamic regulation of DNA hydrogels conjugated with photosensitizers for the development of photodynamic drug-delivery scaffolding systems targeting cancerous or tumor models.

The mechanomodulatory effect of DNA hydrogels has been briefly explored in this work. A thorough understanding of the underlying mechanotransduction pathways and their regulatory elements is vital for a comprehensive understanding of the effects of these stiffness-tunable hydrogels on cells of different lineages. Therefore, we aim to investigate the levels of mechanotransducers, such as YAP, TAZ, FAK, nesprins, actomyosins, and others, in our upcoming work.

We envision a uniform biopolymeric platform for the fabrication of cell-specific ECM-mimicking scaffolds in a scaled-up, high-throughput capacity. The current understanding, combined with the widespread use of DNA-based hydrogels, holds promise for achieving this goal. In response to a specific cellular mechanical need, known and validated DNA motifs can be mixed in a stoichiometric ratio to develop an ECM-mimicking scaffold of desired stiffness.

In our previous work, we demonstrated the design, synthesis, characterization, and applications of soft DNA hydrogels made up entirely of DNA duplex-inspired architectures. In this work, we aim to explore alternative DNA supramolecular motifs that form rigid/ mechanically stronger complexes to create hydrogels. Here, we have demonstrated fourteen unique DNA supramolecular Complexes, where six complexes have truncated blunt ends, acting as our intrinsic controls, making them incapable of forming hydrogel networks. In contrast, the remaining complexes have sticky ends, enabling them to form geometrically defined, intricate networks.

The ssDNA strands are designed through a rational approach while relying on the Watson-Crick base-pairing rule. The strands were enriched in high GC content, where binding was expected/ programmed to be higher (at the ends) compared to the availability of AT-rich regions (cross-over sites), where flexibility of the strands was to be expected. Depending on the complexity of the supramolecular design, the number of ssDNA strands varies. For example, to form DX and PX structures, four strands of ssDNA were utilized in equimolar (1:1:1:1) concentrations to form the complex. The complexes, such as TTs, TQs, TPs, and THs, have the following stoichiometric ratios of ssDNA: 3:1:3 (Three ssDNA in TT), 4:1:4 (Three ssDNA in TQ), 3:2:1:5 (four ssDNA in TP), and 2:2:2:1:6 (five ssDNA in TH), respectively.

Based on the envisioned complex structure design, a 2D framework was first created on the scadNANO website105 (https://scadnano.org/). Based on the above 2D framework (DX, PX, TTs, TQs, TPs, and THs), the sequences were rationally integrated in an iterative manner to maximize thermodynamic stability while minimizing unwanted crossovers and potential to form primer-dimers, secondary loop structures. The pipeline of the design process is illustrated in Fig. 2.b. The ssDNA sequences were then fed to the open-source RNAStructures web server (https://rna.urmc.rochester.edu/RNAstructureWeb/) to predict secondary structure in a pairwise-assembly mode using the DuplexFold Algorithm. The Pair-wise Gibbs free energy values were used to scrutinize the sequences iteratively to arrive at the final refined sequences.

These refined sequences were then synthesized and purified. These sequences, according to their specific architecture type, are pooled in a PCR reaction tube, in the presence of MgCl2 ions, followed by a thermal annealing protocol as reported earlier50,52,53. After gradually cooling down the hydrogels, they are stored at 4 °C until further use.

The hydrogels formed by various ssDNA assemblies are similar in nature yet different in terms of size, shape, structure, and morphology, which makes them excellent for programming cellular behaviours. These DNA hydrogels are characterized by the following techniques.

The EMSA is typically performed on a dense gel system, often with the aid of electrophoresis. In our case, we used a 10% Native PAGE gel prepared with a 30% acrylamide/bisacrylamide solution (29:1), crosslinked with 10% APS and 10 µL TEMED in 5x TAE solution. The freshly prepared DNA Hydrogel samples (2 µL) were then mixed with 1 µL of 6x DNA-loading dye, followed by loading them into the wells for electrophoretic separation. The EMSA was performed in ice-cold TAE buffer to minimize the temperature increase generated during electrophoresis. The powerpack was programmed to run for 90 min at 90 V for efficient separation. The bands of the separated DNA hydrogel samples were stained with EtBr for 15 min under gentle shaking, followed by imaging using the Bio-Rad gel documentation system.

A 10 µL sample of freshly prepared DNA hydrogels (10 µM) was mixed with 1 µL of a 1:1000 dilution of GreenR intercalating dye (Ex/Em = 495 nm/523 nm). The mixed samples were then directly loaded onto a freshly cleaned glass slide, followed by the application of a thin coverslip. The prepared slides were then taken for confocal microscopy-based imaging of GreenR-labelled DNA hydrogels. The images were obtained using a 63x oil-immersion lens in the Leica SP8 confocal microscope. The images were taken at a 512*512-pixel format coupled with z-stacking. The pinhole was set to 1 Airy unit, and imaging parameters, including bit depth, laser power, and detector gain, were kept constant across all experimental conditions within a given study. The images were processed in the open-source Fiji software (ImageJ v1.54p).

The freshly prepared DNA samples were directly drop-cast over a cleaved mica sheet. The samples were stored in a vacuum desiccator for air drying. Once the samples were completely dried, the hydrogels' morphological features were imaged using Bruker's NanoWizard Sense+ BioAFM instrument. The imaging was carried out in tapping mode while using a Scanasyst-Air tip with a 2 nm tip diameter, operating at 75 kHz oscillating frequency in Direct drive mode. The samples were imaged in a 512 × 512-pixel format.

AFM-based Force spectroscopy (AC-mode) was performed using SAA-SPH-1UM tips (Φ = 1 µm, k = 0.25 N/m) over Bruker's NanoWizard sense plus instrument. The scan was performed in a relatively closed-loop mode, measuring a total of 1024 points per 100 µm2 area (32 points × 32 points) while maintaining a constant force of the interacting tip. The force maps were plotted using vertical deflection (nN, Y-axis) and measured Heights (µm, X-axis), and batch-processed by performing an elastic fit using the Hertz/Sneddon model. The output generates a histogram of Young's modulus of 1000 bins of the sample. The average of six histograms was compiled to represent the final graph (Fig. 7).

An oscillatory Amplitude sweep test at 1 Hz oscillation was performed on freshly prepared 200 µL samples of DNA HG (50 µM concentration) using the Anton Paar rheometer model MCR302. An 8-mm geometry probe, a parallel plate, was used to perform oscillatory shear deforming strain on the hydrogel samples. The oscillatory amplitude sweep test was performed across a shear strain range of 0.1% to 100% at 25 distinct measurement points, with a constant frequency of 1 Hz or 6.28 rad/s. Likewise, for the frequency sweep test, the samples were deformed over a varying frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 Hz, while maintaining a constant shear strain of 10%. The geometry used here was an 8 mm parallel plate geometry, while the samples were measured across 25 points throughout the frequency range.

Human retinal pigment epithelial-1 (RPE-1) cells were obtained as a gift from Prof. Ludger Johannes at the Curie Institute, Paris, France. RPE-1 cells were cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 100 IU/mL penicillin−streptomycin (Penstrap) at 37 ⁰C with 5% CO2 and 95% Relative Humidity (RH) levels. The staining of the cells was carried out in Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS) of 1x strength and pH 7.4, as well as serum-free DMEM media.

The effect of DNA hydrogels on RPE-1 cells was analyzed using an MTT-based cytotoxicity assay. In a 96-well plate, all fourteen DNA hydrogel samples, with varying concentrations, along with controls, were coated and incubated overnight. After overnight incubation, RPE1 cells were seeded at a seeding density of 10,000 cells/ well. The cells were allowed to grow over the substrates for 24 h. The old media was decanted and washed with fresh 1x PBS before adding MTT reagent at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. The MTT-added culture plates were then incubated at 37 °C under dark conditions. After four h of incubation, the MTT reagent was removed, and DMSO was added, followed by gentle rocking to dissolve the formazan crystals. The absorbance of the formazan crystals was measured at 562 nm using a portable 96-well plate reader from Byonoy.

The RPE1 cells were cultured until 90% confluency, followed by seeding over 10 mm coverslips coated with different substrates at a seeding density of 10,000 cells/well. The substrate samples included PLL, Collagen-I, Geltrex, and DNA hydrogels at a concentration of 50 µM. After 24 h of incubation, the cells were washed off with PBS, followed by fixing under 4% PFA conditions for 15 min. The remaining PFA was washed off with PBS again after incubation, followed by sequential staining of RPE 1 cells with 1 µg/mL MitoTracker deep red in PBS for 15 min, using the Phalloidin-Alexa488 (in 0.1% Triton X-100) staining protocol as reported in our previous work52. After staining, the fixed cells were washed three times with 1x PBS solution, followed by mounting them on a DAPI-containing Moviol mount and left to dry overnight at 4 °C. The images were captured using a Leica SP8 confocal microscope with a 63x oil immersion objective and optimized laser settings. Fluorophores were excited using the appropriate lasers: DAPI at 405 nm (Diode 405 nm laser), Phalloidin-Alexa-488 (using 488 nm with an Argon laser), and Mitotracker DeepRed (633 nm HeNe laser). The acquired images were analysed using ImageJ software. A minimum of 30 cells was quantified, followed by graph plotting in GraphPad Prism (v. 8.0.2). All the statistical tests were performed using GraphPad Prism. For all the tests, an ordinary one-way ANOVA was performed with **** signifying p-value < 0.0001.

For the study of organellar distribution, the staining was performed after 4% PFA fixation, followed by PBS washing. The staining solution was prepared by adding 1 µg/mL of Mitotracker DeepRed and 1 µL of 1 mM ER tracker Green-BODIPY FL to 1 mL of PBS. In place of PBS, serum-free media could also be used. All staining processes post-fixation were performed by placing the cells over an ice block (4°C refrigerator) to minimize the unbinding of stains from the target site, protein denaturation, and signal loss.After 15 min of staining, the fixed cells were washed three times with 1x PBS, followed by mounting with DAPI-containing Moviol. The Images were taken using a 63x oil immersion objective in the Leica SP8 Confocal microscope, at an airy disc of 1, with a pixel format of 512×512 and z-stacking. The fluorophores were excited using 405 nm (DAPI) by a Diode 405 nm laser, 488 nm (ER Tracker BODIPY-FL) using an Argon laser, and 633 nm (MitoTracker DeepRed) using a HeNe laser. The images were analyzed using Fiji software, and the quantification was carried out using GraphPad Prism. A one-way ANOVA test was performed on a normally distributed dataset of 30 cells per experimental condition. The test statistics show **** significance as p-value < 0.0001.

The confocal data were processed using the free-source Fiji software. The images were first separated into respective channels, then the z-stacks were merged at maximum intensity. Masks were created from the flattened images and subtracted from the original flattened images using the Image Calculator plugin. As a result, the processed images have zero background fluorescence. Cell boundaries were then marked based on measurements of cellular content, including fluorescence, area, and other parameters. These results were transferred to an Excel sheet for quantification and analysis with GraphPad Prism software.

In GraphPad Prism, data such as cell area, fluorescence, roundness, and circularity were compared using one-way ANOVA tests. Before performing the ANOVA, all data sets, each containing 30 cells, were checked for normality. The standard one-way ANOVA was applied without pairing or matching data between groups, assuming equal standard deviations. In all confocal experiments, the control group was the PLL group. The test involved 18 individual groups, with an alpha level of 0.05. A p-value of less than 0.0001 indicated highly significant results.
